Methodology White Paper
Version 1.1

Capex Intent

Composite signal capturing capital‑expenditure momentum through durable goods orders, prioritising ex‑transportation when available.
v1.1 update What changed vs v1.0
  • Data-source documentation expanded (provenance, release cadence, revisions/benchmarking, and vintage tracking).
  • Interpretation section expanded to emphasise real-time uncertainty and revision risk; includes academically grounded guidance.
  • Methodology amendment: durable-goods series are noisy/revised; add smoothing and/or diffusion-index options; publish latest-value table and uncertainty bands.

Abstract

The Capex Intent signal infers forward business‑investment trends from durable goods order data. It combines year‑over‑year and short‑term (3‑month annualised) growth rates for total and ex‑transport durable orders. When the ex‑transport series (ADXTNO) is present, it is prioritised. The composite detects Expansion, Neutral, or Contraction phases in capex cycles.

1. Data

1.1 Primary series

  • DGORDER — Manufacturers’ New Orders: Durable Goods (FRED; source: U.S. Census Bureau M3).
  • ADXTNO — Manufacturers’ New Orders: Durable Goods Ex‑Transportation (FRED; preferred).
  • DGEXORD — Legacy alternate ex‑transport series (fallback if available in your pipeline).

1.2 Provenance & construction

These are monthly nominal series (USD millions) originating from the U.S. Census Bureau’s Manufacturers’ Shipments, Inventories, and Orders (M3) survey and distributed via FRED. Series may be seasonally adjusted depending on the FRED vintage you ingest; confirm units and adjustment flags in metadata.

1.3 Release cadence, revisions & benchmark updates

  • Monthly releases: advance report and full report; near-term revisions to recent months are common.
  • Annual / benchmark revisions: Census benchmarking to annual surveys/economic census can revise history and seasonal factors.
  • Operational risk: scheduled releases can slip (e.g., rescheduling around holidays/government operations); freshness checks should use expected release dates and last-updated timestamps.

1.4 Vintage / real-time considerations (recommended)

If you want stable backtests and defensible “what was known when” analysis, ingest vintages via ALFRED (FRED’s archival/vintage system) rather than only the latest revised history. This enables explicit revision quantification and uncertainty bands in downstream interpretation.

2. Transformations

  1. YoY Growth: g_{12} = X_t / X_{t−12} − 1
  2. 3‑Month Annualised Growth: g_{3mAnn} = (X_t / X_{t−3})^4 − 1

The 3‑month annualised rate accelerates detection of inflection points but increases sensitivity to noise and revisions.

2.1 Smoothing (v1.1 amendment)

Durable‑goods orders are noisy and revised. To reduce whipsaws, compute smoothed versions of growth rates and report both raw and smoothed variants:

  • Simple moving average: e.g., 3‑month SMA of g_{12} and g_{3mAnn}.
  • Exponential smoothing: e.g., EWMA with half-life 2–4 months for short‑term growth; longer for YoY.
  • Winsorisation / robust filters: cap extreme monthly moves prior to annualisation to prevent single prints dominating g_{3mAnn}.

2.2 Diffusion index option (v1.1 “nice-to-have”)

For a more robust capex-intent proxy, build a diffusion index (dynamic factor / PCA summary) across a small basket of related series (e.g., DGORDER, ADXTNO, nondefense capital goods ex‑aircraft if included elsewhere in your library, shipments/unfilled orders where appropriate), then map the factor to regimes. This can reduce reliance on one volatile series.

3. Normalisation

z_{rob}(X)_t = \dfrac{X_t − median(X)_{t,w}}{1.4826·MAD(X)_{t,w}}
Rolling window w = 48 months (minimum 18). Median absolute deviation scaling reduces distortion from outliers.

3.1 Uncertainty bands (v1.1 amendment)

Publish uncertainty bands to communicate revision/noise risk. Recommended implementation:

  • Revision-based bands: using ALFRED vintages, compute the historical distribution of first-to-latest revisions for each series and derive ±1σ (or percentile) bands for g_{12} and g_{3mAnn}.
  • Signal-confidence proxy: map larger expected revisions / larger recent volatility to lower confidence, and surface it alongside the regime label.

4. Composite Construction

Weights adjust automatically depending on data availability:

# If ADXTNO (ex‑transport) is available:
Capex_Intent = 0.4·z(ADXTNO_YoY) + 0.4·z(ADXTNO_3mAnn) + 0.2·z(DGORDER_YoY)
# Otherwise:
Capex_Intent = 0.5·z(DGORDER_YoY) + 0.5·z(DGORDER_3mAnn)

4.1 “Orders ex‑transport” emphasis

Transportation equipment can dominate headline orders in certain months; prioritising ex‑transportation reduces distortion and improves interpretability as a capex proxy.

4.2 Publishing latest-value table (v1.1 amendment)

Each signal run should publish a small table of “what the model saw” (latest observation date, raw levels, growth rates, smoothed growth rates, z-scores, composite, regime, and uncertainty). This improves auditability and downstream debugging.

Latest snapshot (template)
Field DGORDER ADXTNO (if available) Notes
Last observation (YYYY‑MM) {latest_date} {latest_date_ex} Use FRED “last_updated” + expected release schedule for freshness checks.
Level (USD mn) {x_level} {x_level_ex} Nominal; consider deflation if you want real capex signal.
YoY growth {g12} {g12_ex} Report both raw and smoothed.
3m annualised growth {g3m} {g3m_ex} Highly sensitive to monthly noise and revisions.
Smoothing (e.g., 3m SMA) {g12_smooth}, {g3m_smooth} {g12_ex_smooth}, {g3m_ex_smooth} Choose one method and document parameters.
Composite (Capex_Intent) {capex_intent} Include component contributions and confidence.
Regime {Expansion/Neutral/Contraction} See regime rule below.
Uncertainty band {band_low} to {band_high} Based on historical revisions and/or volatility proxy.

5. Regime Classification

If\ C_t > 0.75 → Expansion;\quad |C_t| ≤ 0.75 → Neutral;\quad C_t < −0.75 → Contraction

Thresholds are heuristic; consider calibrating against historical recession/slowdown windows or adapting thresholds to volatility regimes.

6. Implementation (Python)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def robust_z(s, win=48, min_win=18):
    x = pd.to_numeric(s, errors="coerce").astype(float)
    w = max(min_win, min(win, x.dropna().size))
    med = x.rolling(w, min_periods=min_win).median()
    mad = (x - med).abs().rolling(w, min_periods=min_win).median()
    return (x - med) / (1.4826 * mad.replace(0, np.nan))

def ann3(series):
    return (series / series.shift(3))**4 - 1.0

def sma(series, n=3):
    return series.rolling(n, min_periods=n).mean()

# Optional: revision-aware uncertainty bands
# - Use ALFRED vintages to compute revision distributions
# - Then map to bands for g12 and g3mAnn

d = df_durables.copy()

ex_col = None
if "ADXTNO" in d.columns:
    ex_col = "ADXTNO"
elif "DGEXORD" in d.columns:
    ex_col = "DGEXORD"

target_cols = ["DGORDER"] + ([ex_col] if ex_col else [])
for col in target_cols:
    d[f"{col}_YoY"]   = d[col].pct_change(12)
    d[f"{col}_3mAnn"] = ann3(d[col])
    # v1.1 smoothing (example: 3m SMA)
    d[f"{col}_YoY_sma3"]   = sma(d[f"{col}_YoY"], 3)
    d[f"{col}_3mAnn_sma3"] = sma(d[f"{col}_3mAnn"], 3)

# Choose whether to normalise raw or smoothed rates; document the choice.
use_smooth = True

def _pick(base):
    return f"{base}_sma3" if use_smooth else base

if ex_col:
    d["Z_EX_YoY"]   = robust_z(d[_pick(f"{ex_col}_YoY")])
    d["Z_EX_3mAnn"] = robust_z(d[_pick(f"{ex_col}_3mAnn")])
    d["Z_ALL_YoY"]  = robust_z(d[_pick("DGORDER_YoY")])
    d["Capex_Intent"] = (0.4*d["Z_EX_YoY"] + 0.4*d["Z_EX_3mAnn"] + 0.2*d["Z_ALL_YoY"])
    d["Capex_Sources"] = f"DGORDER + {ex_col}"
else:
    d["Z_ALL_YoY"]   = robust_z(d[_pick("DGORDER_YoY")])
    d["Z_ALL_3mAnn"] = robust_z(d[_pick("DGORDER_3mAnn")])
    d["Capex_Intent"] = 0.5*d["Z_ALL_YoY"] + 0.5*d["Z_ALL_3mAnn"]
    d["Capex_Sources"] = "DGORDER only"

hi, lo = 0.75, -0.75
def _regime(v):
    if pd.isna(v): return np.nan
    return "Expansion" if v > hi else ("Contraction" if v < lo else "Neutral")

d["Capex_Regime"] = d["Capex_Intent"].apply(_regime)

# Produce latest snapshot table fields for the webpage output.
latest = d.tail(1).copy()

7. Interpretation (how to read the signal)

7.1 What the data are telling you

  • Expansion: broad-based, above-trend durable-orders momentum consistent with rising business investment commitments.
  • Neutral: mixed/mean-reverting order momentum consistent with baseline capex pace.
  • Contraction: sustained weakening in orders consistent with a downshift in business-investment intentions.

7.2 How much weight to place on a single print

Treat monthly releases as noisy signals, not precise measurements. Revisions and benchmarking mean that “latest history” may differ from what was known in real time; this matters for regime flips and backtests. The model therefore surfaces smoothed rates and uncertainty bands so users can distinguish a durable trend from a one‑off shock.

7.3 What “uncertainty bands” mean

Bands are a pragmatic summary of two risks: (i) data revisions (first release → later vintages) and (ii) measurement noise (month-to-month volatility). When the composite is close to regime thresholds and bands overlap the neutral zone, interpret the regime as lower-confidence and emphasise corroboration with other capex/procyclical indicators.

7.4 Interpreting a diffusion-index variant

If you implement the diffusion-index option, interpret it as a common factor summarising multiple capex-related series. This typically improves robustness under structural change and reduces idiosyncratic series noise, but it can be less transparent without component contribution reporting.

8. Limitations

  • Durable goods orders are volatile and subject to revisions and benchmark updates; smoothing mitigates but does not eliminate noise.
  • Ex‑transportation reduces distortion from aircraft/transport swings but can still be affected by episodic large orders and supply-chain effects.
  • Fixed thresholds (±0.75) are heuristic; calibration or volatility-aware thresholds may improve stability.
  • All series are nominal; if inflation regimes shift materially, a deflated variant may be preferable for some use cases.

9. Academic & practitioner references

  • Real-time/vintage data and revisions: Croushore & Stark (2001, 2003); Croushore (2011).
  • Diffusion indexes / dynamic factors for forecasting: Stock & Watson (1998; 2002).
  • Diffusion-index statistical properties: Pinto, Sarte & Sharp (2020).

Use these to ground interpretation (revision risk, robustness, and factor-based smoothing). Keep citations in your public write-up consistent with your house style.

© 2026. Methodology reference for the Capex Intent signal (v1.1).
